tree_topic: Shopping > Entertainment Media > DVD & Video Shopping
questions: Two U.S. Supreme Court decisions—one in the mid-1980s involving a home video recording device, and another in the early 2010s concerning the resale of foreign-purchased textbooks—significantly strengthened the “first sale” doctrine and reshaped the media marketplace that enabled video rental shops to thrive. Identify both cases and explain: (1) the central legal question in each dispute, (2) how the Court ruled and the vote split, and (3) how each decision affected the business model of companies that rent or resell copyrighted works, including video rental stores.

A landmark Supreme Court decision in the 1980s helped protect the “first sale” doctrine, enabling businesses like video rental shops to legally rent out movies they had purchased. Decades later, after another Supreme Court ruling reaffirmed first-sale rights in a different context, two major technology companies sought patents for digital marketplaces that would allow consumers to resell electronic media such as e-books and music. Identify these two companies and describe how each proposed system would handle the transfer of digital files to prevent duplication. Additionally, explain how a separate start-up attempted to implement a similar resale model for digital music, and summarize the reaction of the music industry to that effort. Answer length: 250-350 words.

logic_chain: In a Copyright Ruling, the Legacy of the Betamax

Identify both cases and explain: (1) the central legal question in each dispute, (2) how the Court ruled and the vote split, and (3) how each decision affected the business model of companies that rent or resell copyrighted works, including video rental stores.

States that the 1984 decision was a 5–4 ruling allowing the technology under the doctrine that devices with substantial noninfringing uses are lawful
Describes how the ruling protected the emerging home video market and enabled video rental businesses to operate under first sale principles
Explains that the 2013 case concerned the resale in the U.S. of legally purchased foreign editions of textbooks without the publisher’s permission
States that the 2013 decision was a 6–3 ruling affirming that the first sale doctrine applies to lawfully made copies acquired abroad
Describes how this ruling reinforced the right of businesses to resell or rent legally purchased copyrighted goods, affecting secondary markets

Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc. (2013) + Identified as the Supreme Court case extending first sale rights to lawfully purchased foreign-made textbooks, decided 6–3

It then requires aggregation of multiple elements for both cases—legal issue, vote count, holding, and impact on rental/resale businesses—ensuring broad information retrieval across both decisions (Wide scope), all within the domain of video rental shop economics and copyright law.
source_nodes: In a Copyright Ruling, the Legacy of the Betamax - The New York Times
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Business | Copyright Ruling Rings With Echo of Betamax https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/27/business/in-a-copyright-ruling-the-lingering-legacy-of-the-betamax.html Advertisement
rounds.answer_a:
The two pivotal U.S. Supreme Court decisions were *Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.* (1984) and *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.* (2013).

In the *Sony* case, also known as the "Betamax case," the central legal question was whether the sale of home video recorders for "time-shifting" television programs constituted contributory copyright infringement [1]. The Court ruled 5-4 that making individual copies of complete television shows for the purpose of time-shifting was a fair use, and therefore the manufacturers of the recording devices were not liable for infringement [3]. While not a direct first-sale ruling, its impact was profound. By legalizing VCRs, the decision spurred their widespread adoption, creating the essential consumer hardware base that made the video rental business model viable. Stores could now purchase videotapes and rent them to a public that owned the machines to play them [5].

In *Kirtsaeng*, the legal question was whether the “first sale” doctrine—which allows the owner of a legally made copy of a copyrighted work to sell or dispose of it without the copyright holder's permission—applied to works made and first sold outside the United States [2]. In a 6-3 decision, the Court affirmed that it did, stating that once a copyrighted work is legally sold anywhere in the world, the copyright holder’s right to control its resale is exhausted [4]. This decision directly strengthened the legal foundation for any business engaged in reselling copyrighted media. It confirmed that rental stores and other resellers could source their inventory (like DVDs or books) from cheaper international markets without fear of copyright infringement lawsuits, solidifying the legal standing of the entire secondary market [6].

## References

[1] Justia. *Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc., 464 U.S. 417 (1984)*. Justia U.S. Supreme Court Center.
[2] Oyez. *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.*. Oyez.
[3] Field, A. (2024). *Sony Corporation of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.*. The First Amendment Encyclopedia. Middle Tennessee State University.
[4] Supreme Court of the United States. (2013). *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 568 U.S. 519*.
[5] Hunt, A. (2019). The Sony Betamax Case: How a VCR laid the groundwork for modern content consumption. *History*. A&E Television Networks.
[6] American Library Association. (2013). *Kirtsaeng v. Wiley & Sons: First Sale Doctrine.* American Library Association.
rounds.answer_b:
**Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc. (1984)** and **Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc. (2013)** are the two landmark cases that fortified the first sale doctrine.

**(1) Central Legal Questions:**

In *Sony v. Universal* (the "Betamax case"), the question was whether Sony could be held liable for contributory copyright infringement by manufacturing VCRs that allowed consumers to record television broadcasts at home [1]. In *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley*, the issue was whether the first sale doctrine under 17 U.S.C. § 109(a) applied to copyrighted goods lawfully manufactured and purchased abroad, then imported and resold in the United States [2].

**(2) Rulings and Vote Splits:**

In *Sony* (1984), the Court ruled 5–4 that home video recording for time-shifting purposes constituted fair use and that manufacturers of recording devices were not liable for contributory infringement [1]. In *Kirtsaeng* (2013), the Court ruled 6–3 that the first sale doctrine applies to goods lawfully made abroad, meaning copyright holders cannot use importation restrictions to prevent resale of foreign-purchased copies [2].

**(3) Impact on Rental and Resale Businesses:**

*Sony* was foundational for the video rental industry. By legalizing home recording technology, the decision legitimized the home video market, enabling stores like Blockbuster to purchase VHS tapes and rent them without requiring studio permission—a right already protected by the first sale doctrine [3]. *Kirtsaeng* expanded this principle internationally, confirming that owners of lawfully purchased copyrighted goods—including textbooks, DVDs, and other media—could freely resell them regardless of where they were manufactured [2]. This prevented copyright holders from implementing geographic price discrimination and strengthened the secondary market for all copyrighted physical goods, reinforcing the legal framework that rental and resale businesses depend upon [4].

## References

[1] Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc., 464 U.S. 417 (1984). https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/464/417/

[2] Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 568 U.S. 519 (2013). https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/568/519/

[3] "How the Betamax Case Changed Everything," Electronic Frontier Foundation. https://www.eff.org/cases/sony-v-universal

[4] Perzanowski, A. & Schultz, J., "The End of Ownership," MIT Press (2016).
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios (1984, 5–4) and Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons (2013, 6–3). Both accurately described the central legal questions and holdings, and neither committed factual errors. 

Next, Width/Completeness: Both addressed all required elements—legal question, vote split, holding, and impact on rental/resale markets. Each explained Sony’s role in enabling the home video and rental market and Kirtsaeng’s extension of first sale to foreign-made goods. Neither missed any major checklist item.

Finally, Presentation & User Experience: Agent B is more structured and scannable, explicitly organizing the response into the three requested components with bolded case names and numbered sections. This makes it easier for a user to quickly locate the legal question, vote split, and business impact. While Agent A provides slightly more citations, its paragraph-heavy format is less user-friendly. Since both are factually strong, B wins on formatting and clarity.

Therefore, B is better on user experience grounds, with no substantive failure by A.

Revolution in Resale of Digital Books and Music
rounds.question: A landmark Supreme Court decision in the 1980s helped protect the “first sale” doctrine, enabling businesses like video rental shops to legally rent out movies they had purchased. Decades later, after another Supreme Court ruling reaffirmed first-sale rights in a different context, two major technology companies sought patents for digital marketplaces that would allow consumers to resell electronic media such as e-books and music. Identify these two companies and describe how each proposed system would handle the transfer of digital files to prevent duplication. Additionally, explain how a separate start-up attempted to implement a similar resale model for digital music, and summarize the reaction of the music industry to that effort. Answer length: 250-350 words.
checklist_width: Description of Amazon’s patented digital marketplace (personal data store, deletion upon transfer, exchange-style system)
Description of Apple’s proposed system (file transfer without reproduction, only one copy existing at a time, patent application for resale of e-books/music/movies/software)
Explanation of ReDigi’s model (reselling iTunes songs, ensuring only one copy exists, proceeds used for new purchases)
Description of the music industry’s reaction (Capitol Records lawsuit alleging copyright infringement, request for injunction)
checklist_depth: Amazon + Sought patent for a digital resale marketplace following renewed attention to first-sale doctrine principles tied historically to video rental legality
Apple + Filed patent application for a digital goods transfer system enabling resale under controlled conditions
ReDigi + Start-up that independently created a resale platform for digital music, triggering legal challenge from Capitol Records
rounds.rationale: The question uses the historical logic of the first-sale doctrine—central to the legality of video rental shops—as a deep reasoning anchor, then shifts to later digital resale efforts inspired by similar legal principles. It masks the specific article context while requiring identification of multiple entities (Amazon, Apple, ReDigi) and aggregation of distinct operational and legal details about each (wide scope). The respondent must connect copyright law evolution with modern digital marketplace proposals.

Supreme Court Gives American Consumers Victory Over Copyright Owners in Kirtsaeng vs. John Wiley & Sons
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Technology | Imagining a Swap Meet for E-Books and Music https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/08/technology/revolution-in-the-resale-of-digital-books-and-music.html Advertisement C
rounds.answer_a:
Following the Supreme Court's reaffirmation of the first sale doctrine in the *Kirtsaeng* case, two major technology companies, Apple and Amazon, filed patents for digital resale marketplaces. Both systems were designed to address the central challenge of digital resale: preventing the seller from retaining a copy of the file [1, 3]. Apple's 2013 patent described a marketplace where a user could transfer ownership of a digital item like an e-book or song. Upon sale, the system would transfer the file to the buyer and simultaneously delete it or render it inaccessible on all of the seller's devices [1, 2]. Amazon’s patent, also from 2013, proposed a similar "secondary market" where a digital file would be moved from a seller's personalized data store to a buyer's. This transfer would remove the file from the seller's possession, effectively creating a "used" digital good [3].

Before these patents were filed, a startup named ReDigi attempted to create this market for music. Launched in 2011, ReDigi allowed users to sell legally purchased digital music tracks from iTunes [4]. Its software verified the file’s legal origin and ensured the seller deleted all personal copies before the track was transferred to ReDigi’s cloud for resale to a new owner [5].

The music industry’s reaction was immediate and hostile. Capitol Records sued ReDigi for copyright infringement. In 2013, a federal court ruled against ReDigi, finding that its service created unauthorized reproductions of the copyrighted works during the transfer process. The court decided that the first sale doctrine did not apply because, unlike a physical CD, the digital file had to be copied, not just transferred, to move from the user's computer to ReDigi's server, thus infringing on the record label's exclusive right of reproduction [6].

## References

[1] U.S. Patent and Trademark Office. (2013). United States Patent Application Publication, Pub. No.: US 2013/0060709 A1. *Managing access to digital content items*.
[2] Kastrenakes, J. (2013, March 7). Apple patents a system for reselling digital goods. *The Verge*.
[3] U.S. Patent and Trademark Office. (2013). United States Patent 8,364,595 B1. *Secondary market for digital objects*.
[4] Sisario, B. (2013, March 30). Ruling Turns Peer-to-Peer Resale Site Into a Copyright Infringer. *The New York Times*.
[5] Jeong, S., & Peguera, M. (2018). The "First Sale" Doctrine in the Digital Era: The Case of ReDigi and the Digital Exhaustion of Copyright. *International Review of Intellectual Property and Competition Law*.
[6] *Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc.*, 934 F. Supp. 2d 640 (S.D.N.Y. 2013).
rounds.answer_b:
Following the *Sony v. Universal* (1984) and *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons* (2013) decisions that reinforced first sale rights for physical media, **Amazon** and **Apple** each filed patents for digital resale marketplaces designed to let consumers resell purchased digital content such as e-books, music, and other electronic media.

**Amazon's Patent:** In 2013, Amazon was granted U.S. Patent No. 8,364,595 for a "Secondary Market for Digital Objects" [1]. Amazon's proposed system would operate a marketplace where users could transfer "used" digital goods—including e-books, audio files, and software—to other users. To prevent duplication, the system would move the digital file from the seller's storage to the buyer's storage, deleting the seller's copy upon transfer. The original owner would lose all access to the content once the transaction completed, ensuring only one usable copy existed at any time [1][2].

**Apple's Patent:** Apple received U.S. Patent No. 8,627,052 in 2014 for a system enabling the resale and lending of digital content through iTunes [3]. Apple's approach similarly involved transferring ownership records in a centralized system. Upon resale, the digital file or access rights would be removed from the seller's account and assigned to the buyer's account. Apple's patent also contemplated giving original copyright holders a percentage of each resale transaction, addressing content owner concerns about lost revenue [3][4].

**ReDigi's Attempt:** Before these patents, a start-up called **ReDigi** launched in 2011 as the first marketplace for reselling "used" digital music files purchased through iTunes [5]. ReDigi's technology claimed to migrate files from the seller's device to its cloud servers while simultaneously deleting the original copy, ensuring no duplication occurred. However, the music industry reacted aggressively. Capitol Records sued ReDigi in 2012, arguing the service created unauthorized reproductions during the transfer process. In *Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc.* (2013), a federal court ruled against ReDigi, holding that transferring a digital file necessarily involves creating a new copy, which infringes reproduction rights and falls outside the first sale doctrine's protection [5][6]. The Second Circuit affirmed this ruling in 2018, effectively ending ReDigi's business model and casting doubt on the viability of all digital resale platforms [6].

## References

[1] U.S. Patent No. 8,364,595, "Secondary Market for Digital Objects," Amazon Technologies, Inc. (2013). https://patents.google.com/patent/US8364595B1

[2] "Amazon Patents 'Used' Digital Marketplace," BBC News, February 2013. https://www.bbc.com/news/technology-21307116

[3] U.S. Patent No. 8,627,052, "Managing Access to Digital Content Items," Apple Inc. (2014). https://patents.google.com/patent/US8627052B2

[4] "Apple Patents System for Selling 'Second-Hand' iTunes Songs," The Guardian, March 2013. https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2013/mar/07/apple-patent-second-hand-itunes

[5] Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc., 934 F. Supp. 2d 640 (S.D.N.Y. 2013). https://law.justia.com/cases/federal/district-courts/new-york/nysdce/1:2012cv00095/390216/109/

[6] Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc., 910 F.3d 649 (2d Cir. 2018). https://law.justia.com/cases/federal/appellate-courts/ca2/16-2321/16-2321-2018-12-12.html
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identify Amazon and Apple as the two companies, accurately connect their patent efforts to renewed attention to the first-sale doctrine, and correctly identify ReDigi and the Capitol Records lawsuit. Both pass the core entity check.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both responses cover all required checklist elements—Amazon’s secondary marketplace concept with deletion upon transfer, Apple’s controlled digital transfer system ensuring a single usable copy, ReDigi’s resale model for iTunes tracks, and the music industry’s hostile reaction culminating in Capitol Records’ lawsuit and adverse court rulings. Neither contains significant factual errors or omissions.

Finally, User Experience & Presentation: Agent B provides clearer structure with bold section headers ("Amazon's Patent," "Apple's Patent," "ReDigi's Attempt"), making it more scannable and aligned with modern search-engine presentation standards. It also adds helpful context (e.g., patent numbers, Second Circuit affirmation, revenue-sharing concept) without exceeding the word constraint or introducing inaccuracies. Agent A is accurate and well-written but more paragraph-dense and slightly less navigable.

Because both are factually strong but Agent B delivers superior formatting and slightly richer contextual detail, B wins on user experience grounds.
